text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Priya Nair 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair, 

Alex Turner, DOB: 21-07-1996, Hospital No: K837491 NHS No. 1029384756  
Flat 3, 22 Market Row, Brixton, London SW9 8LD  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with frequent focal aware and focal impaired awareness seizures; levetiracetam previously ineffective; currently trialling lacosamide with partial response

Aetiology: 

Unclear; no structural lesion identified on MRI to date; normal birth and development per patient

Other Medical Problems: 

Mild eczema to forearms managed with emollients; no known drug allergies

Management Plan: 

1) Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and lacosamide 100 mg twice daily. Monitor for dizziness and diplopia.  
2) Arrange ambulatory EEG (48 hours) to characterise events occurring during work shifts.  
3) Serum levels and U&E/LFTs in 6 weeks; consider up-titration of lacosamide to 150 mg twice daily if tolerated and seizures persist.  
4) Workplace safety advice given: avoid proximity to deep-fat fryers and open grill during high-heat rush periods where feasible; colleague awareness plan in place.  
5) DVLA guidance reviewed; patient not currently driving.  
6) Review in clinic in 8 weeks or earlier if events escalate.

This 29-year-old commercial kitchen line cook describes brief, stereotyped episodes occurring on the hot line during peak service. He reports "sz *three/d" on average during evening shifts, more commonly when moving between the grill and fry station in a high-heat, fast-paced environment. Semiology begins with a rising epigastric sensation and metallic taste, followed by right-hand posturing and brief behavioural arrest lasting 20–40 seconds. He sometimes experiences subsequent confusion for several minutes but denies generalised tonic–clonic activity. No injuries reported, though he has stepped back abruptly from the range on two occasions. Triggers appear to include heat, flashing order screens, missed meals, and sleep deprivation from split shifts. 

He has focal epilepsy. Prior trials included levetiracetam up to 1500 mg twice daily without benefit and with irritability; carbamazepine previously avoided due to concern about interaction with kitchen shift contraception of partner not applicable; patient preference to avoid sedation. Since introducing lacosamide alongside lamotrigine four weeks ago, the intensity of events has reduced; frequency remains as above. He hydrates and takes regular breaks when permitted; employer has agreed to reassign him to prep station during periods of higher seizure activity, which he finds helpful. Neurological examination today is unremarkable. 

I will update you following ambulatory EEG and bloods, and we will adjust medications accordingly with a view to improving control while maintaining function at work. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: He reports "sz *three/d" on average during evening shifts. This provides a numeric frequency of three per day. There are no other conflicting frequencies mentioned. The note about partial response states frequency remains as above, confirming the same rate currently.
